title: Crash Course Creators
synopsis: Starling: Two BBA students race a deadline to build an app that helps new artists publish stories and short films. Chaos, code, and creativity collide.
body:
Page 1 — Panel 1
Narration box (top): Semester project: build an app for new artists to publish stories and short films. Two weeks to go.
Wide shot of a cluttered dorm room. Posters, guitar, coffee cups, and sticky notes on the wall reading “MVP,” “Pitch — 72 hrs,” and “No Sleep Club.”
Aaryan (lean, optimistic, hoodie) slumps at a laptop. Meera (practical, pencil behind ear) sketches wireframes on a notepad.

Aaryan (muttering): We have an idea, a team, and… exactly zero code that works.
Meera (deadpan): And a deadline that smells like panic.

Panel 2
Close-up of the laptop screen: a chaotic collection of tabs — “React tutorial,” “Firebase docs,” “How to make a login.”
Aaryan (cheery bravado): It's fine. We’ll do a clean user journey: sign up, upload story, preview, publish. Boom.
Meera (pointing at notes): You forgot authentication, video encoding, moderation, server costs...
Aaryan (interrupting): Okay, fine — boom, boom, boom.

Panel 3
Enter roommate Ravi, wearing earbuds, holding two instant noodles.
Ravi: Why are you still awake? Exams ended last week.
Meera: We’re building Starling — a platform for upcoming storytellers to publish and make short films.
Ravi: So, like... TikTok meets film school?
Aaryan (proud): Exactly. But better.

Panel 4
Meera whispers to Aaryan, anxious.
Meera: Funding pitch in eight days. Mentor’s review in five. Prototype in three.
Aaryan (smiling weakly): We thrive under pressure.
Meera (tense): We’re about to be very, very cooked.

Page 2 — Panel 1
Cut to college campus courtyard. The mentor stands under a banyan tree — Professor Iyer, mid-50s, warm but sharp glasses.
Professor Iyer (smiling): How’s Starling?
Aaryan (energetic): Almost there, sir.
Meera (blunt): We’ve made… enthusiastic progress.
Professor Iyer (raising eyebrow): Enthusiastic?

Panel 2
Professor Iyer gestures toward a tablet showing a wireframe with scribbles and sticky notes.
Professor Iyer: Show me the product.
Aaryan flips through the app screens — all placeholders and “lorem ipsum.”
Professor Iyer (sotto): Hmm. A set of promises without delivery.

Panel 3
Meera (defensive): We have a concept video, a landing page, and a great Instagram.
Professor Iyer (kind): But where is the user experience? Where is the problem you’re solving for an artist who has zero tech help?
Aaryan (mouths): Oops.

Panel 4
Professor Iyer puts a hand on each of their shoulders.
Professor Iyer: Build one thing that works. Not everything that looks impressive. Think like a storyteller: hook, conflict, resolution. Start there.

Page 3 — Panel 1
Montage panels (small): They interview a film student, a spoken-word poet, and a short-filmmaker. Each complains: uploading is hard, formats break, no feedback, distribution is a nightmare.
Voice-over boxes (Meera): Artists want an easy upload, simple previews, and a place where viewers leave useful notes, not trolls.

Panel 2
Back in the dorm, Meera draws a simple flow: Upload -> Auto-convert -> Preview -> Publish -> Community Feedback.
Aaryan (typing): Auto-convert… we can use open-source encoders and a cloud function.
Meera (counting on fingers): Moderation? Community curation? Minimal viable feature list — comments, like, curator flag.
Aaryan (eyes bright): And a creator dashboard for analytics — views, watch time, feedback highlights.

Panel 3
Ravi appears, holding a makeshift sign: “Beta Testers Wanted!”
Ravi: I can get film club people and my cousin’s theatre group.
Meera (relieved): Good. Real users, finally.

Panel 4
Their first beta test: a shaky, excited short film uploads successfully. The app converts it; a clean preview plays on a tablet.
Meera (exhales): It works.
Aaryan (grinning): One small miracle.

Page 4 — Panel 1
Cut to late-night debugging montage: Aaryan wrestling with a server error, Meera calming a frustrated tester on video call, Ravi organizing snacks and motivational quotes.
Narration box: Two days until mentor review.

Panel 2
At 2 AM, the app crashes during a bulk upload. Error logs scroll like a horror movie.
Meera (pale): We lost the converter queue.
Aaryan (frantic): Rollback! Requeue! Re-architect!
Ravi (muttering while snacking): Maybe it wants a nap.

Panel 3
Professor Iyer calls.
Professor Iyer (calm): How are things?
Meera (honest): We hit a wall.
Professor Iyer (firm): Then tell the truth in your demo. Show the working parts and the plan to fix the rest. Creativity isn't a flawless product; it's a created solution.

Panel 4
They take a breath and rewrite the demo script: start with a passionate filmmaker’s problem, show the working upload-preview-publish loop, then outline the roadmap for scaling and moderation.

Page 5 — Panel 1
Demo day in a small auditorium. A banner reads: Final Project Showcase.
Aaryan and Meera present nervously. The opening slide is a filmmaker’s face, eyes hopeful.
Aaryan (narration while demo plays): Meet Tara. She made a ten-minute film on her phone but couldn’t share it without losing quality or getting lost in noise.

Panel 2
Live demo: Tara’s short uploads, converts, preview plays crisply. The audience leans in.
Meera (explaining): We focused on what matters — creators can publish without codec drama, get feedback, and build an audience.

Panel 3
Professor Iyer watches, expression unreadable.
Aaryan (whispering to Meera): She liked the sound design of the preview.
Meera (whispering back): Focus!

Panel 4
After the demo, a panel of judges asks sharp questions about moderation, costs, and scalability.
Meera answers with clarity and a roadmap; Aaryan explains technical choices; Ravi promises to build community partnerships.

Page 6 — Panel 1
Weeks later, their inbox pings: “Seed Grant — shortlisted.”
Aaryan, Meera, and Ravi jump around the room, hugging, spilling coffee. The apartment looks like confetti exploded.
Meera (laughing): We didn’t just survive the deadline.
Aaryan (grinning): We made an actual thing people can use.

Panel 2
Montage: A young poet uploads a spoken-word piece; a student filmmaker gets comments that improve their edit; an open-mic night promoted through the app fills a small theater.
Narration box: Starling starts small. Real creators, real feedback, tiny bursts of magic.

Panel 3
Final shot: Evening on the college roof. The three sit with their laptops open to Starling’s creator dashboard showing live users.
Professor Iyer joins them, hands them a steaming chai.
Professor Iyer (soft): You built one clear thing, and that made the rest possible.
Meera (looking at the dashboard): The app is still messy. There are bugs. But people are making work.
Aaryan (content): Progress, not perfection.

Panel 4
Close-up of the Starling logo on the laptop: a simple bird icon mid-flight.
Caption (bottom): Some stories begin with a deadline. Others begin because someone finally gave creators a place to be heard.
